title: Single-question page vs text input vs textarea vs error summary
summary: Use a single-question page to structure one decision or answer per screen, text input or textarea for the field itself, and error summary for recovery after submission errors.
decisionRules: Prefer a single-question page when one answer or decision deserves a focused screen with heading, hint, answer control, validation, Continue, and Back behavior.
Use text input within a single-question page when the answer is short, freeform, and fits on one line.
Use textarea within a single-question page when the same focused page asks for a paragraph, explanation, comment, or other multi-line answer.
Use an error summary when a submitted question page has one or more errors that need page-level announcement and links back to the invalid control.
Do not call every text field a single-question page; the pattern is the page structure, route, heading, progress position, and navigation contract around the answer.
Do not split tightly related fields onto separate pages when they answer one conceptual question, such as a date with day, month, and year fields.
Prefer a single-page form when users benefit from scanning and editing a short set of low-risk fields together before submitting.
Prefer a multi-step form when the journey has sections or tasks, and use single-question pages as individual steps inside that broader flow.
Prefer a wizard when the sequence is highly guided, stateful, or branching with strict next-step dependency; do not use wizard chrome for a simple isolated question.
Keep Back and Continue behavior predictable: returning to the question restores the answer, while Continue validates only this page's question before moving on.
failureModes: A page asks several unrelated questions, so the heading does not tell users what answer is expected.
A single text input is mistaken for the pattern while the page has no focused heading, hint, Back, Continue, or recovery state.
A simple standalone question is wrapped in wizard navigation that suggests unnecessary progress and future commitments.
A question page loses the saved answer after Back, validation, or route changes.
Related controls that belong to one answer are split across pages, forcing users to remember context.
A submitted error appears only beside the control without any page-level recovery when focus lands at the top.

sources.note: GOV.UK question-page guidance supports one question per page, question-as-heading structure, explanatory text, and grouping fields that answer the same question.
GOV.UK form-structure guidance supports one thing per page while distinguishing a question from a single form field.
GOV.UK good-question guidance supports plain-language questions and reframing complex questions before changing field controls.
